title: St. James the Apostle (Alpheus) — St. Dorotheus of Tyre & St. Marcian the Martyr
primary_saint: St. James the Apostle Son of Alpheus, St. Dorotheus the Bishop of Tyre the Elder Martyr, and St. Marcian the Martyr of Iconium
narrative:
On the fifth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the feast of the holy Apostle James the son of Alpheus, known in the tradition as James the Less to distinguish him from James the son of Zebedee, and sometimes identified with James the brother of the Lord — an identification disputed in the tradition but theologically rich in either case, since it connects the obscurer of the two apostle-Jameses to the leadership of the Jerusalem church, the mother of all churches, and to the profound epistle that bears the name of James and that is the New Testament's most insistent document of the social dimension of faith. James son of Alpheus is one of the saints whose very obscurity in the Gospel narrative is the tradition's point: he appears in the lists of the Twelve and almost nowhere else, yet the tradition assigns him a mission to Persia and a martyrdom by crucifixion or by clubbing, and the Church honours him not because of what the Gospels tell us about him but because of what the apostolic commission made him: one of the twelve foundations of the holy city.

The theology of James son of Alpheus in the Ethiopian tradition is inseparable from the theology of the Twelve as a corporate body: the apostolic college is not an assembly of individually distinguished heroes but a community of the called, in which each member's significance derives not from his personal prominence but from his being chosen. The obscure apostles — James of Alpheus, Simon the Zealot, Thaddaeus, Matthias — are the tradition's rebuke to the cult of celebrity and the theology of the visible: the Church is built not only on the Peters and the Pauls, not only on those whose names and faces and words are preserved in ample detail, but on the unnamed and the unquoted and the barely-remembered, on the twelve foundations of which only some are famous and all are necessary. James of Alpheus is every faithful Christian who has served without recognition and without record, whose name is in the Book of Life even if it is barely in the history books.

Saint Dorotheus of Tyre is a figure of extraordinary longevity and extraordinary suffering: a bishop who lived to the age of one hundred and seven, surviving multiple persecutions across multiple reigns, serving the church of Tyre through decades of turmoil, outlasting emperors and heresies and waves of persecution, and finally dying as a martyr — clubbed to death for refusing to sacrifice — during the persecution of Julian the Apostate, the last pagan emperor. Dorotheus is the tradition's icon of the endurance of the Church across time: the man who was one hundred and seven years old when he was finally martyred had seen more of Christian history than almost any other single witness, had lived through the transition from persecution to tolerance under Constantine and back toward persecution under Julian, and his death at the hands of the last persecuting emperor is the tradition's seal on an era: the very old man who would not sacrifice was the last chapter of the Roman persecution of Christians.

Saint Marcian the Martyr of Iconium stands alongside the century-old bishop as the tradition's reminder that martyrdom is distributed without regard to age, experience, or prominence: Marcian died young, in the same spirit but in different circumstances from the ancient Dorotheus, and his death for the faith in Iconium — the city of Paul and Barnabas, the city where the apostles had preached and where a church had existed since the first century — places him in apostolic company. Iconium is itself a sacred geography in the tradition: the city where Paul and Barnabas were expelled but where a church survived, where Thecla received the word that changed her life, where the faith took root in rocky soil and endured. Marcian dying in Iconium is the tradition's testimony that the seed planted by Paul and Barnabas in that city bore fruit across the centuries in the blood of those who came after them, and that the martyrs of each age are the harvest of the apostolic sowing. Amen.
